sourceMovie.title: maboroshi
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: maboroshi (2023) stuck because it commits to its animation premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Mari Okada's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The animation and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Ride Your Wave
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If maboroshi clicked for you, Ride Your Wave (2019) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and drama, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Illusionist
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If maboroshi clicked for you, The Illusionist (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and romance, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after maboroshi?
faq.answer: Try Ride Your Wave, The Illusionist, The Boy and the Heron—each shares DNA with maboroshi while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is maboroshi?
faq.answer: maboroshi (2023) is best described as Animation, Drama, Romance, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is maboroshi worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this animation lane.
